cwe: CWE-307 - Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts

threat.title: Weak Password Policy Enforcement Enabling Account Compromise
threat.description: When applications fail to enforce strong password requirements (complexity, length, common password checks), attackers can compromise user accounts through brute force attacks, password spraying, or credential stuffing. Weak password policies are responsible for 81% of hacking-related breaches and enable attackers to gain unauthorized access to sensitive systems and data across Spring Boot, Express.js, and Django applications.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies target application with user registration/login functionality
2. Discovers weak password policy through trial registrations (e.g., accepts 'password123')
3. Compiles list of target accounts from public sources, data breaches, or user enumeration
4. Launches automated credential stuffing attack using common password lists (rockyou.txt, leaked credentials)
5. Successfully authenticates to 5-10% of accounts using weak/common passwords
6. Leverages compromised accounts to access sensitive data, perform privilege escalation, or establish persistence
prerequisites: Application allows weak passwords (< 8 chars, no complexity, common passwords)
No rate limiting or account lockout mechanisms on authentication endpoints
No monitoring or alerting on suspicious login patterns

cve_references.description: IBM Common Licensing does not require strong passwords by default, making it easier for attackers to compromise user accounts
Progress Telerik Report Server prior to 2024 Q3 allows password brute forcing due to weak password requirements
HCL Compass vulnerable to insecure password requirements enabling easier account compromise

real_world_breaches.impact: Millions of customers had personal and financial information stolen through credential stuffing attack exploiting weak/reused passwords
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Weak password policies allowed users to set easily compromised passwords; lack of MFA on customer accounts

real_world_breaches.impact: 165 organizations compromised using stolen credentials from accounts lacking MFA; only required valid username and password
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Weak password requirements combined with no MFA enforcement allowed credential-based attacks

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Application accepts trivial passwords like '12345678' enabling account takeover via brute force

security_research.title: 2024 Password Statistics: 81% of Breaches from Weak Passwords
security_research.key_finding: 81% of hacking-related breaches stem from weak or reused passwords; 49% of all data breaches involve compromised passwords

security_research.title: Specops 2025 Breached Password Report
security_research.key_finding: 24 billion passwords exposed in 2022 alone - 65% increase from 2020; weak passwords cause 30% of global data breaches

mitigations.title: Spring Boot 3.x Password Validation with Custom Validator
mitigations.description: Implement comprehensive password strength validation in Spring Boot using custom validators, passay library for complexity rules, and integration with HaveIBeenPwned API to prevent use of compromised passwords.
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are issued, managed, verified, revoked
pci_dss: Requirement 8.2.3 - Passwords must meet minimum complexity
Requirement 8.2.4 - Change user passwords at least every 90 days
validation_steps: Test registration with passwords < 12 characters (should fail)
Test with passwords lacking uppercase/lowercase/digit/special (should fail)
Test with common passwords like 'Password123!' (should fail)
Verify BCrypt work factor is 12+ by checking hash generation time (>200ms)
Confirm passwords are hashed in database (start with $2a$ or $2b$)

mitigations.title: Express.js 4.x Password Strength with zxcvbn and Rate Limiting
mitigations.description: Implement password strength checking using zxcvbn library (developed by Dropbox) for realistic password strength estimation, combined with express-rate-limit to prevent brute force attacks.
iso27001_controls: A.9.2.4 - Management of secret authentication information
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are issued, managed, verified
PR.PT-4 - Communications and control networks are protected
pci_dss: Requirement 8.2.3 - Passwords must meet minimum complexity
validation_steps: Verify rate limiting triggers after 5 failed login attempts
Test registration with common passwords (should reject)
Verify helmet middleware sets security headers (X-Frame-Options, etc.)
Test that user enumeration is prevented (same error for invalid user/password)

mitigations.title: Django 4.x/5.x Custom Password Validators with django-password-validators
mitigations.description: Implement comprehensive password validation using Django's built-in validators extended with custom rules, including checks against compromised password databases and complexity requirements aligned with NIST SP 800-63B.
iso27001_controls: A.9.2.4 - Management of secret authentication information
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are issued, managed, verified
PR.AC-7 - Users, devices, and other assets are authenticated
pci_dss: Requirement 8.2.3 - Passwords must meet minimum complexity
validation_steps: Run: python manage.py check to verify configuration
Test registration with password < 12 chars (should fail)
Verify 5 failed login attempts triggers account lockout
Check password hash in database starts with $argon2id$
Test that ComplexityValidator rejects passwords without special chars
Verify HaveIBeenPwned API integration with known breached password

indicators: Multiple failed authentication attempts from single IP address
High volume of failed logins across multiple accounts (password spraying)
Successful logins after multiple failures (successful brute force)
Authentication attempts with common passwords in HTTP request bodies
Account lockouts triggered for multiple users simultaneously
Unusual geographic locations for login attempts
High rate of password reset requests

log_queries.query:
// Detect brute force authentication attempts
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where ResultType != "0"  // Failed logins
| summarize 
    FailedAttempts = count(),
    UniqueUsers = dcount(UserPrincipalName),
    Users = make_set(UserPrincipalName)
    by IPAddress, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where FailedAttempts > 10
| order by FailedAttempts desc
| project 
    TimeGenerated, 
    IPAddress, 
    FailedAttempts, 
    UniqueUsers,
    Users,
    Severity = case(
        FailedAttempts > 50, "High",
        FailedAttempts > 20, "Medium",
        "Low"
    )

index=auth_logs sourcetype=application_logs
| where action="login" AND status="failure"
| bucket _time span=5m
| stats count as failed_attempts, dc(user) as unique_users, values(user) as users by src_ip, _time
| where failed_attempts > 10
| eval severity=case(
    failed_attempts > 50, "High",
    failed_attempts > 20, "Medium",
    1=1, "Low"
  )
| table _time, src_ip, failed_attempts, unique_users, users, severity
| sort -failed_attempts

sequence by source.ip with maxspan=5m
  [authentication where event.outcome == "failure"] with runs=10
| stats failed_count = count(), 
        unique_users = cardinality(user.name),
        users = values(user.name)
  by source.ip
| where failed_count > 10

fields @timestamp, @message, sourceIPAddress, userIdentity.userName, responseElements.ConsoleLogin
| filter eventName = "ConsoleLogin" and errorCode = "Failed authentication"
| stats count(*) as failed_attempts by sourceIPAddress, bin(5m)
| filter failed_attempts > 10
| sort failed_attempts desc

tools.name: fail2ban
tools.usage: Automatically blocks IPs with multiple authentication failures

compliance: NIST CSF (PR.AC-1 - Identity and Credential Management)
